title: Grazing rotation planning for a small flock
subtitle: Dividing land to prevent poaching, parasite build-up, and pasture degradation.
excerpt: Keeping the same animals on the same patch of ground continuously degrades the pasture, builds worm burdens, and produces poached muddy ground that is hard to recover. Rotating two or three paddocks (even on a small site) breaks the parasite cycle, allows grass recovery, and reduces the labour of managing a muddy field every winter.
sourceNotes: AHDB Beef & Lamb, pasture management for sheep, ahdb.org.uk. Farming Advisory Service, rotational grazing guidance. DEFRA Countryside Stewardship management guidance for livestock grassland.

glossaryTerms.definition: A rotational system where the grazing area is divided into multiple smaller paddocks. Animals graze one paddock while others rest and regrow. The rest period breaks the parasite lifecycle and allows grass to recover to optimal height before the next grazing.

Damage to soil structure and grass sward caused by animal hooves in wet conditions. Poached ground loses drainage capacity, supports fewer grass species, and becomes dominant with rushes and docks over successive seasons.

content.text: does not require large acreage. Even dividing a half-acre field into three sections and rotating a small flock of four to six sheep improves the situation substantially compared with continuous grazing. The rest period for each paddock is what does the work.

For sheep and goats, a paddock needs at least 42 to 56 days of rest from the point of grazing to the point of regrazing to allow infective larvae from faeces to die off on the sward. Larvae hatch within a week of faecal deposition but die quickly in dry, sunny conditions; wet or overcast conditions extend survival. A 6-week rotation cycle is the practical minimum for meaningful parasite control benefit.

Temporary electric fencing is the most practical way to divide an existing field without permanent infrastructure investment. Electric fence stakes and a single strand of poly-wire divide a paddock into segments in under 30 minutes. Move the fence when the grass in the grazed section is down to 5 to 8 cm; sheep and goats graze lower than this readily and produce significant soil compaction on bare ground from

A simple sketch map of the paddocks with the dates of entry and exit written onto it is enough to track the rotation. A paddock that was grazed on 1 April should not receive sheep again before about 12 May at the earliest. Tape the map to the inside of the feed shed door where you will see it daily.

Land that has not carried sheep or goats for at least a year is clean grazing, carrying minimal infective larval burden. This is the most effective paddock for lambs in their first summer when worm resistance is lowest. If possible, reserve one paddock through the previous season as a clean area for the current year's young stock.
